data.title: Barron's 100 Sustainable Dividend Dogs Fetch 1 Ideal February Buy Of 31 "Safer" Picks
data.description:
Foreword

This article is based on 100 top sustainable companies as determined by Calvert Research and Management's annual review of more than 230 Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG) performance indicators, such as workplace diversity, data security, and greenhouse-gas emissions, as reported in the February 21, 2025 edition of Barron's Weekly.

How Barron’s Ranked the Companies

“To build this list of most sustainable companies, Barron’s worked with Calvert, a leader in ESG investing. Starting with the 1,000 largest publicly traded companies by market value, Calvert whittled the 1,000 largest publicly traded companies, not including real estate investment trusts, down to 100 by looking at five weighted categories—planet, workplace, customer, community, and shareholder—according to 230 performance indicators from rating companies including Institutional Shareholder Services, MSCI, Sustainalytics, and Thomson Reuters Asset4. It then used sector experts to refine the rankings by analyzing how well companies are moving toward their sustainability targets.

A total of 28 topics are considered across the five categories. For example, “community” includes access to medicine, food sourcing and supply chain, human rights, and animal welfare. “Planet” covers toxic reduction and elimination, resource efficiency, energy use and efficiency, and greenhouse-gas emissions. Calvert assigned a score of zero to 100 in each category, based on company performance. All companies on the list scored above the bottom quartile in each category that is relevant to their businesses.

Now if they can just sustain it.”

— Barron's editors

Any collection of dividend stocks is more clearly understood when subjected to yield-based (dogcatcher) analysis. These 100 publicly traded most sustainable dogs are perfect for the dogcatcher process. Here, February 13 data focused on 81 dividend payers. The full list of 100 is posted in the Afterword of this article.

Happily, 9 of 81 ordinary, and 1 of the 31 ‘Safer’ dividend-paying Sustainable companies, live up to the Dogcatcher ideal of showing annual dividends from a $1K investment exceeding their single share prices. As of 2/13/26, the 9 are: Robert Half (RHI), The Campbell’s Co (CPB), General Mills Inc (GIS), Hormel Foods Corp (HRL), Regions Financial Corp (RF), Graphic Packaging (GPK), LKQ Corp (LKQ), Keurig Dr Pepper Inc (KDP), and Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE). Of the 9 only Regions, showed up as “safer”.

Many first-time investors regard thie ideal of annual dividends from a $1K investment exceeding their single share prices as a buy signal or, at least, an invitation to look-closer. the “Safer” ranking is given to stocks whose free cash flow yield exceeds their dividend yield, Paying dividends from cash flow is safer than using borrowed money.

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Analysts Estimated 17.46% To 42.16% Net Gains From Ten Top ESG Companies To February 2027

Four of ten top 2025 ESG company stocks by yield were among the top ten gainers for the current year based on analyst 1-year target prices. (They are tinted gray in the chart below). Thus, the yield-based forecast for these February ESG top dogs was graded by Wall St. Wizards as 40% accurate.

Estimated dividends from $1000 invested in each of the highest-yielding stocks plus their aggregated one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, supplied the data points. Note: target prices from less than two analysts were not counted. Thus, ten probable profit-generating trades projected to February 13, 2027 were:

Barrons Top Ten Sustainable February Dogs By Net Gains

Robert Half was projected to net $421.57, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 11 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 12% less than the market as a whole.

Automatic Data Processing (ADP) was projected to net $334.59 based on dividends, plus the median of target estimates from 17 brokers, less transaction fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 15% less than the market as a whole.

Best Buy Co (BBY) was projected to net $262.04, based on the median of target price estimates from 27 analysts, plus annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 44% more than the market as a whole.

LKQ Corp was projected to net $233.40 based on the median of target estimates from 9 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 9% less than the market as a whole.

MetLife (MET) was projected to net $205.50 based on dividends, plus the median of the target price estimates from 17 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 23% less than the market as a whole.

Graphic Packaging was projected to net $200.95 based on the median of target estimates from 11 analysts, plus its last dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 38% under the market as a whole.

Skyworks Solutions (SWKS) was projected to net $187.10 based on the median of target prices from 26 analysts, plus annual dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 33% over the market as a whole.

Keurig Dr Pepper was projected to net 179.63 based on a median of target price estimates from 16 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 66% under the market as a whole.

Hewlett Packard Enterprise was projected to net $178.63, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 33% over the market as a whole.

Hormel Foods was projected to net $174.61 based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 10 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 67% less than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 23.78% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these ten stocks. These gain estimates were subject to average risk/volatility 12% under the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

50 Barron's ESG Dogs Showed February Analyst Target Gains

50 Barron's ESG Dogs Showed February 1yr Total Return Gains

50 Barron's Sustainable Dogs Reveal February Yields

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top Barron's Sustainable Dogs By Yield For February

February 2026 Barron's Sustainable stocks represented six of eleven Morningstar sectors.

One industrials member took the first slot, Robert Half. [1]. A consumer discretionary representative nabbed second place, Best Buy [2].

Then one financial services stocks occupied the third place, Prudential Financial (PRU) [3].

Thereafter five consumer staples (defensive) stocks occupied fourth, to seventh, and tenth slots: Campbell’s [4], General Mills [5], Hormel Foods [6], Kimberly Clark (KMB) [7], and Clorox (CLX) [10].

A technology sector member placed eighth, Skyworks Solutions [8].

Finally, a lone utilities sectorstock placed ninth, Eversource Energy (ES) [9], to complete the top ten February 2026 list of Barron's Sustainable ESG 2025 stocks by yield.

Barron’s Sustainable 100 Costs per Dividend Dollar as of 2/13/26-7

The above chart reveals 81 dividend-paying Barron’s Sustainable dogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is displayed the cost per dividend-dollar for each stock.

The least expensive dog of these Sustainables is Robert Half Inc at $10.50 per dividend dollar, and the most expensive is NVIDIA at $4,566.21 per dividend dollar.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Eighteen of Eighty-one Dividend Paying Sustainable Dogs Show Negative Free-Cash-Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That means, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. Eighteen dividend paying Sustainable stocks so-infected in February are Robert Half, Hormel, Kimberly-Clark, Eversource Energy, Graphic Packaging, New Jersey Resources, Excelon, Consolidated Edison, Public Service Enterprise, American States Water, Air Products, Texas Instruments, IDACORP, International Flavors, Nucor, Constellation Energy, and Ormat. These are considered unsafe to buy.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, uncertainty, deals, tariffs, and artificial intelligence, too.

Price Drops or Dividend Increases Could Get All Ten Top-Yield Sustainable Stocks Back to "Fair-Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, four in the top ten are already ideally priced. Beside those four, already in the ideal zone, six more top-yield stocks are within $56 of getting there.

The alternative, of course, could be that these companies raise their dividends or split their shares. That may be too much to ask in these highly disrupted, inflationary, recessionary, yet cash-rich, and volatile times. Mr. Market is far more effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts, just watch and buy when your targeted stock price moves to a sweet spot.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 13.79% To 33.63% Top Ten Sustainable Upsides To February 2027; (32) Five -0.49% to -7.49% Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 12.96% Advantage For The 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 Sustainables As of February, 2027

Ten-top Sustainables were culled by yield for this monthly-update. Yield (dividend / price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten Sustainables selected 2/13/26 by yield, represented six of eleven sectors. Energy, Health Care, Materials, Real Estate, and Technology missed the top-ten cut.

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten Highest-Yield Dividend Sustainables (34) To Deliver 15.04% Vs. (35) 13.31% Net Gains by All Ten Come February 13, 2027

Analyst 1-year targets indicated $5000 invested as $1,000 in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten Dividend Sustainables delivered 12.96% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The second lowest-priced top-ten yielding stock, Robert Half showed top analyst-estimated gains of 42.16%.

The four lowest-priced top-yield Sustainable dogs for February were: Hormel; Robert Half; Campbell’s; General Mills; Skyworks, with prices ranging from $23.78 to $62.10.

Five higher-priced Sustainable top-yield dogs as of February 13 were: Best Buy; Eversource Energy; Prudential; Kimberly-Clark; Clorox, with prices ranging $65.80 to $125.94.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As post-holiday froth in the market cools and the feckless Fed-fueled slide and dithering presidential deals gain momentum to open 2026, with the exception of a few magnificent meme stocks, look for most of the top ten Sustainables to become Fair-priced… slowly, but surely, or suddenly.

Afterword

Buy 1 Ideal of 31 ‘Safer' February Sustainables

Early February 2026, 31 of the 100 Sustainables were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields. YCharts 2/13/26 data showed one was fair-priced, Regions Financial

How Sustainable 'Safer' Dividend Vs. Price Results Produced "Fair Price” Results For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that one dividend from $1K invested in our top ten 'safer' dividend Sustainable conforms to the dogcatcher ideal standard of being equal to (or greater than) the price of a single share. That ideal dog was Regions Financial.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) equal to or greater than the single share price of each stock; a so-called, “fair price.”

A market correction of 81.6% could put all ten share prices of these “safer” survivors above or equal to their dividend payouts from $1k invested.

The 1 ‘Safer’ Ideal November Sustainable Dividend Dog

Regions Financial Corp stood out by (1) not being redlined as "cash-poor" stock; (2) not showing negative one year returns; (3) being market priced less than the dividend yield from $1K invested.

There is, of course, another way for dividends from $1K invested to exceed the price of a single share of stock. That would be for the boards of directors these companies to raise dividends or do stock splits to move listings to the dogcatcher ideal level. That’s a lot to ask with all the corporate funds doing so much “good” elsewhere.

Footnotes

Barron’s Sustainable Rankings as of 2025

Calvert Research and Management scored the 1,000 largest publicly traded companies across 230 environmental, social, and governance, or ESG, performance indicators. The above chart lists the 2025 100 top Sustainable dogs in alphabetical order after their 2025 and 2024 rankings.The right columns, reveal score, total return, February price, market value, and 2025 estimated price/earnings.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher Sustainable dog updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace). The 2026 top 100 update is due out later this month from Barron’s.

The net-gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" Sustainable dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational and educational purposes only and should not be construed to constitute investment advice. Nothing contained herein shall constitute a solicitation, recommendation or endorsement to buy or sell any security. Prices and returns on equities in this article except as noted are listed without consideration of fees, commissions, taxes, penalties, or interest payable due to purchasing, holding, or selling same.

Graphs and charts were compiled by Rydlun & Co., LLC from data derived from Indexarb; YCharts; finance.yahoo.com; analyst mean target price by YCharts. Open source dog art from dividenddogcatcher.com

data.title: February 2026 Trading Outlook: Fiscal Flows, Bank Credit, And Fed Policy
data.description:
Introduction

This report applies the sectoral balance, real estate cycle, and fiscal flows framework to assess how fiscal and monetary dynamics are shaping risk asset performance. With inflation cooling, unemployment rising, and the Federal Reserve signaling a shift in policy, the interplay between fiscal expansion and monetary restraint is increasingly consequential.

Sectoral Balances Overview

The following table tracks monthly changes in sectoral balances, velocity, and acceleration—metrics that help forecast market direction.

Fiscal Flow Breakdown

January is seasonally a weak month for fiscal flows, and this last one has been weaker than normal. The overall result is a net private sector surplus of $122+ billion, and this is positive for risk asset prices but down from last month, which brings negative velocity and lower acceleration effects.

Going into detail: The $122+ billion private sector funds surplus came from a $97 billion injection of funds from the federal government. This injection was augmented by a healthy $100+ billion of credit creation by the banking sector and a debit of $-75 billion from the external sector.

Bank credit growth within the various credit types is shown in the chart below.

While credit is still rising and adding overall liquidity to the market there are signs of alarm now. The chart below shows that debt in default is trend up now.

The ascent in debt default has started into the recession of 2030 and is a repeat of the symptoms that accompanied the land led recession of 2006-2010. Expect defaults to rise from here and peak in 2030.

External Sector Impact

The external sector is projected to extract about $75 billion from the private domestic sector in exchange for imported goods and services. While recent trade policies have reduced the trade deficit, the net effect remains a drag on domestic liquidity, but the real goods and services imported add to productivity and standard of living. One could say the fiscal deficit has been reduced in return for a corresponding reduction in the real goods and services surplus.

Market Implications

The chart below shows the sectoral balance data plotted in nominal terms. The calculation is federal government spending, or G plus the external sector (X and usually a negative factor). The result is how much money enters or leaves the private domestic sector, or P, an accounting identity true by definition.

Last month the model predicted a flat to positive finish and in reality ended in negative territory.

Next month the model is predicting a similar result, with the SPX finishing at best flat as a series of weak private domestic sector balance results undermine risk markets. One can see in the chart above how the green line is tracking downwards month over month.

Fiscal Policy Outlook

The table below shows the total federal government withdrawals from their accounts at the Federal Reserve Bank. A withdrawal by the federal government is a receipt/credit for the private sector and, therefore, a positive for asset markets.

Federal outlays in December broke an all-time record, with over $3.5 trillion being spent. This has very positive effects on monetary velocity and acceleration and, for that matter, risk asset markets. The deficit overall was nonetheless relatively small due to the spending being taxed back out again almost as fast as it flowed in, leaving only a modest private sector surplus behind.

January is still a strong spend at over $3.1 trillion however the deficit was small indicating that even though a lot was spent most of it was drained out again from Federal taxation and the like.

Fiscal milestones coming up:

This month there was a major federal tax collection on the 15th of January, and seasonally January is one of the weakest months for fiscal spending.

A large treasury interest payment was made in early February and it is not unusual for market to rally into the middle of February. This undoes some of the damage done by the tax extraction.

From the 20-year seasonal stock market chart shown at the end of this article, one can see the market can rally into February for this reason. After the February rally the chart shows the market generally drops into March due to the back-to-back tax collections and weak fiscal flows at this time of year. Last year the seasonal drop was very severe as it timed with the Trump Liberation Day Tariff announcement where weak liquidity met negative sentiment that amplified the seasonal move downward.

At the White House, a federal government shutdown was averted at the 11th hour with a quick ad hoc funding bill

Monetary Policy Outlook

At its January 2026 meeting, the Federal Reserve kept interest rates unchanged, maintaining the federal funds rate in the 3.5%–3.75% range. This decision aligned with broad market expectations and continued the Fed’s cautious stance as it monitored economic data.

The Fed officially ended QT on December 1, 2025, marked by a $13.5 billion liquidity injection via repurchase operations — one of the largest since the pandemic.

Ending QT signals the Fed is no longer shrinking its balance sheet and is instead preparing for a more accommodative stance.

The green line on the chart below has started to tick back up again. The balance sheet is growing again. This takes bonds off the banks and gives them bank reserves in return with which they can buy more risk assets.

This is very positive for risk asset market inflation and will help the real estate market peak as forecast for this cycle [mid-2026] and also fuel further stock market excesses.

The housing market index has been revised by the Federal Housing Finance Agency and the revision shows the housing index is making all time highs after the results for 2025 were revised downward. Prior to this time the market appeared to have made a high in early 2025 and then failed to make a higher second high in 2026 which from a charting technical analyst point of view is a classic sign of weakness.

Fed Rate Stance

The following chart plots key indicators used to estimate what the Fed might do next and has proven very predictive.

Market Signals

The federal funds rate [FFR] is led by a multitude of co-factors, and these are listed and discussed in the table below:

Indicator / Line What It Measures Historical Behaviour What the Current Chart Suggests
S&P 500 – 1‑year % change (forward 1 month) (Red) Equity market momentum Stocks typically lead economic turns and Fed policy shifts Momentum appears to be rolling over → signals slowing conditions and supports a pause or easing in Fed rates
CPI – 1‑year % change (Light Purple) Inflation Fed policy lags inflation trends CPI is falling → reduces pressure for further hikes and supports a plateau in policy rates
10‑Year Treasury Yield – 1‑year change (Green) Bond market expectations for growth and inflation Declines often precede Fed rate cuts The line is turning down → bond market is pricing in slower growth and lower inflation
GDP – 1‑quarter growth rate (annualised) (Magenta) Short‑term economic growth GDP growth peaks and troughs lead Fed moves by several quarters Growth appears to be softening → indicates weakening economic activity and future easing bias
Fed Funds Rate (FFR) (Blue) Actual Fed policy rate Lags all other indicators Currently elevated and flattening → likely at or near the peak of the cycle
10‑Year Treasury Yield (level) (Brown) Long‑term interest rates Fed typically follows the direction of long‑term yields Yield appears to be declining → reinforces expectation of future Fed cuts
Initial Unemployment Claims (Black) Labour market stress Claims rise before recessions and before Fed cuts Claims appear to be bottoming or rising → signals labour softening and supports easing
Non‑farm Payrolls – 1‑quarter % change (Dark Green) Employment growth Slows before Fed cuts Trend is weakening → indicates cooling labour market and reduced need for restrictive policy

The data is signalling that the Fed is at or near the top of the rate cycle, and the next major move is likely downward.

Given POTUS has made is clear in policy statements that he wants lower interest rates and that the Fed has a new chairman to replace outgoing Chairman Powell it is likely new Chairman Warsh will be inclined toward lower rates. General market conditions are pointing towards steady to lower rates as well as the table above shows.

This will help at the short end of the curve and enable banks to borrow short and lend long and maintain a healthy spread however the long end of the curve is market led and remains stubbornly high. The mortgage rate remains stubbornly high as the chart below shows and is what the majority of voters are looking at as this drives the cost of accommodation whether it be for rent or mortgage payment.

The chart above shows the housing market with a multitude of co-factors that are summarised in the table below.

Series

Line Colour

What It Measures

Mortgage Rate (30‑yr FRM, inverted)

Cost of borrowing for homebuyers (inverted so falling rates appear as rising values)

Inverted line turning upward → actual rates easing

Lower rates will lead a broad housing rebound; earliest turning point

Forward indicator of future construction activity

Appears to have bottomed and is turning upward

Early sign of a new construction cycle beginning

Stabilising and following permits

Starts will rise next, confirming the upturn

GDP component tied to homebuilding and renovations

Deeply negative but flattening

Contraction ending; will soon add to GDP growth

SPDR S&P Homebuilders ETF

Bottomed earlier and trending upward

Markets already pricing in stronger future earnings

The real estate cycle normally ends once mortgage debt has reached a peak enticed by easy lending and the rates are raised and lenders squeezed and over extended. At present rates are falling and the housing market is resurging so there is more time left in the current cycle.

Global Macro Trends

At the world macroeconomic level, we have the G5 chart below.

This is what the G5 chart is telegraphing quarters in advance:

Line Colour Series Name What It Measures What the Chart Shows (Historical Behaviour) What It Is Forecasting (Forward View)
Black Grayscale Bitcoin Trust ETF (RH scale) Price of Bitcoin via GBTC Moves closely with global liquidity conditions. Peaks when liquidity expands, falls when liquidity contracts. Forecast suggests another upswing in BTC as global liquidity (G5 balance sheets + fiscal spending + China credit impulse) turns upward.
Green (dashed) China Credit Impulse (High Frequency) (RH scale) Rate of change in Chinese credit creation Historically leads global risk assets by several months. When China accelerates credit, global assets rise shortly after. The forward projection shows a rising impulse, signalling a near‑term boost to global risk assets (BTC, ETH, equities).
Purple 4‑week lag of USD→Bitcoin (Bitstamp) / 1000 Liquidity‑adjusted BTC indicator Tracks BTC price with a short lag; rises when USD liquidity improves. The projected rise indicates BTC should continue trending upward as liquidity improves.
Orange 4‑week lag of USD→Ether (Bitstamp) / 1000 Liquidity‑adjusted ETH indicator Similar to BTC but often more sensitive to liquidity swings. Forecast shows ETH following BTC higher as systemic liquidity expands.
Brown 1‑year % change in G5 Government Expenditures (China, US, Eurozone, Japan, Switzerland) Fiscal spending growth Acts as the “carrier wave” of global liquidity. When fiscal spending grows, risk assets trend higher. The projection shows fiscal spending rising again, implying a supportive macro backdrop for risk assets through 2026–27.
Pink MSCI World Index (RH scale) Global equities Closely tracks the combined effect of G5 fiscal + G5 central bank liquidity + China credit impulse. The forward path shows global equities rising into 2026–27 as liquidity improves.
Light Blue G5 Central Banks’ Aggregate Balance Sheet (in USD) Total assets held by the Fed, ECB, BoJ, PBoC, SNB Expansions correlate strongly with rising risk assets; contractions correlate with corrections. The forecast shows balance sheets expanding again, signalling a broad liquidity tailwind for global markets.

The forecast shows a synchronized upswing across all risk assets into 2026–27.

Because:

  • G5 fiscal spending is rising
  • G5 central bank balance sheets are expanding
  • China’s credit impulse is turning up

This combination historically produces strong rallies in global risk assets.

Conclusion & Investment Outlook

The 20-year seasonal averages shown in the chart above points to markets rising into February and falling lower into the middle of March due to the lagged effect of weak fiscal flows caused by back to back Federal taxation in December and January.

data.title: Fastly: The Rebound Rally Is Here, And It's Just Getting Started
data.description:
If any investors are looking at the so-called "SaaSpocalypse" and are afraid that once growth stocks lose momentum they can never recover, Fastly, Inc. (FSLY) is a great example of the opposite. The content delivery network (CDN) was one of the hottest trades during COVID, and then slower growth and weak customer retention rates broke its rally. Recently, however, Fastly has just delivered incredibly strong Q4 results that featured a jump in customer expansion and revenue growth rates.

Quietly, small-cap Fastly has been one of the best-performing tech stocks of the year, with its share price nearly doubling since the start of January. And yet, thanks to its low valuations, Fastly is just beginning to correct several years of being in oversold territory - and in my view, the stock has ample room to run higher.

I last wrote a buy rating on Fastly in November, when the stock was trading around $10 per share. Since then, shares of Fastly have leaped ~80%, making it an outlier as a winner in my portfolio of small- and mid-cap tech bets that have been floundering this year as AI fears build. At the same time, Fastly has fully justified these gains through accelerating revenue, improving retention rates, robust backlog buildup, and healthy margin expansion. I'm reiterating my buy rating here.

It wouldn't be an overstatement to say that Fastly is a company that is completely overhauling its business model and sales execution. One of the core ways in which Fastly is doing this is by expanding beyond its core CDN mission and transforming into a full-stack internet services company that is benefiting from the AI surge. The chart below showcases its three main categories of products and their contribution to overall revenue:

Fastly's core content delivery and video content streaming products are still generating double-digit growth for the company as end-customer usage improves. But we note as well that Fastly has started to add compute capabilities, with AI companies using Fastly Compute for inference, as well as observability/uptime features that position Fastly as a veritable competitor to Datadog (DDOG) and New Relic. Though small at a single-digit percentage of revenue, these new services have the potential to catalyze Fastly's next major leg of growth.

We note that while Fastly's total customer base is shrinking (mostly due to churn from smaller, lower-priority accounts), its enterprise traction is growing. The chart below showcases the company's blue-chip customers across industries, including the likes of JetBlue (JBLU), Duolingo (DUOL), and Wayfair (W).

In my view, Fastly is just at the tip of a favorable transformation, and the stock market is transitioning its viewpoint on the stock from a previous "bargain basement" rating to a company that is benefiting from AI tailwinds. Stay long here and ride the post-earnings surge higher.

Q4 Download

Fastly's fourth-quarter update (and bullish outlook for FY26) was the best earnings print for the company since it fell from post-COVID highs. Take a look at the Q4 earnings summary below:

Revenue grew 23% y/y to $172.6 million, substantially outpacing Wall Street's $161.3 million (+15% y/y) expectations by an eight-point margin. As shown in the chart below, this is Fastly's fourth straight quarter of acceleration. Growth improved 8 points y/y from 15% growth in Q3, which in turn improved from 12% and 8% growth in the prior two quarters. In 2024, Fastly grew at just a 7% pace.

Yes, easier comps are factoring into Fastly's growth acceleration here, but the majority of the outperformance is driven by strong attach rates of new products and Fastly's growing "platformization" strategy, which is encouraging customers to aggregate more services under Fastly's umbrella and spend more on the platform. This is clearly demonstrated by the company's net retention rate climbing to 110%, a four-point sequential improvement as shown in the trended chart below:

Yet another indicator of long-term strength is the fact that Fastly's remaining performance obligations (RPO, or essentially backlog) grew at a stunning 55% y/y pace to $353.8 million. This is a signal that customers are comfortable investing in and building on the Fastly platform for the long haul.

Per CEO Kip Compton's remarks on the Q4 earnings call regarding recent sales strength:

In the fourth quarter, network services grew 19% year-over-year, outpacing market growth. This was attributable to stronger-than-expected event performance and larger customers directing traffic to our platform due to their prioritization of network stability, performance and resilience. Our go-to-market motion is operating with increased rigor and clarity. At the same time, our new product launches, especially in security, have allowed us to deliver more business outcomes for our customers, enabling us to grow faster than the market [...]

In Q4, we continued investment in our platform strategy to drive multiproduct adoption and build upon our cross-sell momentum. We're investing heavily in security and resilience with the latter becoming top of mind for customers in recent months."

As an infrastructure platform, Fastly relies on economies of scale to drive profitability, and we note that greater customer spending has helped Fastly to boost its pro forma gross margins to 64.0%, a jump of 120 bps sequentially versus Q3 and 650 bps y/y versus Q4 of last year.

Adjusted EBITDA more than tripled y/y to $35.0 million in Q4, reflecting a healthy 20.3% margin that improved 12 points from just 7.9% in the year-ago Q4.

Valuation, Risks And Key Takeaways

At current post-earnings share prices near $18, Fastly trades at a market cap of $2.73 billion. We note the company has $361.8 million of cash and $361.9 million of debt on its balance sheet, or essentially a net-zero cash balance - so its enterprise value is essentially equivalent to its market cap.

For FY26, Fastly has guided to $700-$720 million in revenue, or 14% y/y growth (maintaining roughly the same pace as 15% y/y growth for full-year FY25).

This positions Fastly's valuation at 3.8x EV/FY26 revenue. Considering the company's adjusted EBITDA is just starting to climb to a margin in the ~20s alongside gross margin expansion, and Fastly's recent acceleration in growth, we think there is still plenty of opportunity for Fastly to drive an upward re-rating.

Of course, there are risks at play as well that we need to consider. Fastly's recent growth has come from customer upsells and expansion, but customer counts have remained rather stagnant. That said, the company is heavily targeting new account growth in FY26.

All in all, I see more upside than downside for Fastly, even after its sharp recent surge. Stay long here and keep riding Fastly higher.

data.title: ValuEngine Weekly Market Summary And Commentary
data.description:
Weekly Market Recap - Week Ending February 13, 2026

Volatility continues to define the early weeks of 2026, with markets swinging back and forth in short one- to two-day cycles. While headline indexes have struggled to establish sustained momentum, sector rotation remains active beneath the surface. Investors are increasingly reallocating capital away from extended mega-cap growth names and into selective value, dividend, and defensive sectors. Utilities, Real Estate, and Consumer Staples posted notable weekly gains, while Technology and Financials experienced pressure. Against this backdrop, several international and technology names have delivered strong 30-day performance, reinforcing the importance of disciplined stock selection driven by valuation and predictive analytics.

In the below tables, we use major ETFs as a proxy for some major indexes as well as each of the sector groups into which we divide the overall markets. Tracking these over time provides a more defined picture of the US markets than simply tracking major indexes. This is followed by notable individual stock movers over the past month, and finally, our full strategy outlook.

Strategy Note

2026, thus far, has been characterized by one- or two-day swings back and forth. Underneath it all, the global stock market continues to focus on underlying fundamentals and relative valuations, using positions in the Magnificent 7 as sources of funds.

Although earnings season is about two-thirds complete, there are still some major companies scheduled to report this week in addition to Walmart (WMT). Along with Walmart, rated 3 (Hold) by ValuEngine, three major e-tailers are scheduled to report. Only one, Carvana (CVNA), earns our top rating of 5 (Strong Buy). The other two, Etsy (ETSY) and Booking Holdings (BKNG), are rated 3 (Hold). Venerable manufacturer Deere Inc. (DE) is also rated 3.

Two other major companies receive favorable ratings: Newmont Corp. (NEM) is rated 5 and Analog Devices (ADI) is rated 4 (Buy). However, Newmont is considered 26% overvalued by our valuation model, while ADI is considered among the worst 10% of all companies we follow in valuation terms, at 73% overvalued. Although we expect ADI to outperform in the short term, in a market focused on value, any misstep or perceived weakness is likely to result in a sharp decline.

Despite a high forward P/E of 42, our valuation model rates Carvana as 6% undervalued. This is partially due to an earnings growth rate of 60% with a price-to-earnings-growth ratio (PEG) of just 1.14. Be aware that CVNA is highly volatile with a beta of 3.45, signifying that statistically a 1% move in the broader market is often accompanied by a 3.5% move in CVNA, both upward and downward. Also, Carvana pays no dividend. In addition to income, the commitment to pay a dividend is seen by the markets as a sign of long-term stability.

In the most recent full analytic blog, published a few days ago, we made a list of twenty U.S.-listed companies along with 20 ADRs that are all rated 5 or 4 by our predictive model and are also rated undervalued by our valuation model. Most of them are also dividend payers with P/E ratios of 20 or less.

The three companies we especially want to highlight, particularly for conservative investors, are AES Corp. (AES), Garrett Motion (GTX), and Barclays PLC (BCS). In addition to all being rated 5 (strong buy), all are calculated as undervalued by our model, all pay dividends, and all have relatively low betas.

AES is one of the largest worldwide suppliers of clean electricity directly to major corporations. It has a generous yield along with a beta below 1.0 and potential for strong earnings growth. Garrett Motion is a spin-off of U.S.-based Honeywell Inc. It specializes in advanced automotive boosting technologies, including passenger and commercial vehicle turbochargers, electric boosting systems, and hydrogen fuel cell compressors. It pays a dividend of 2.0% and also has a beta of less than 1.0.

Although its corporate headquarters have been moved to Switzerland and much of its production is done in China, Garrett Motion’s primary listing is on the NASDAQ. Barclays PLC is among the world’s largest global banking and financial services companies, founded in the UK in 1896. It has a dividend yield of 2.4% and a beta of less than 1.0. We believe these three stocks are stable enough to consider for potential additions or substitutions in most equity portfolios.

data.title: Alger Mid Cap Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Strategy

Primarily invests in growth equity securities of U.S. mid cap companies identified through our fundamental research as demonstrating promising growth potential. Seeks long-term capital appreciation.

Benchmark

Russell Midcap Growth Index

Highlights

  • During the fourth quarter of 2025, the largest portfolio sector weightings were Information Technology and Industrials. The largest sector overweight was Information Technology and the largest sector underweight was Consumer Discretionary.
  • The Information Technology and Utilities sectors contributed to relative performance while Health Care and Communication Services were among sectors that detracted from relative performance.

Market Environment

U.S. equities ended the fourth quarter on a firm footing, with the S&P 500 Index returning 2.7%, extending the steady grind upward that characterized 2025, after the tariff announcements. Investor confidence was supported by a familiar trio: corporate earnings that came in better than expected, a Federal Reserve (Fed) that pushed further into an easing stance, and a macroeconomic backdrop that — despite mounting cross-currents — remained resilient in the aggregate. Market sentiment also benefited from improving clarity on trade policy, including an extension of the U.S. – China truce and selective tariff relief aimed at easing consumer affordability pressures.

At the same time, the quarter was defined by widening dispersion beneath the index-level surface. The market's biggest tailwind — continued investment tied to artificial intelligence (AI) — was increasingly accompanied by scrutiny around AI infrastructure bottlenecks, sources of financing, and whether the AI theme could deliver returns commensurate with the capital deployed. Macroeconomic risks also remained in play: labor market softening became harder to ignore, consumers appeared more cautious and bifurcated across income cohorts, and housing stayed under pressure. Complicating the read-through, a prolonged government shutdown reduced economic data visibility, amplifying debate over inflation and employment signals just as global central-bank dynamics leaned less uniformly supportive. Nevertheless, U.S. equities reinforced a constructive baseline for the S&P 500 Index, where Health Care was the top performing sector, while Real Estate was the weakest.

During the quarter, we continued to observe secular themes that we believe are creating attractive investment opportunities—corporations are digitizing their operations, cloud computing is growing and supporting innovation, and AI is at an inflection point, potentially enabling significant increases in productivity. Moreover, U.S. business spending appears to be accelerating, driven by a confluence of factors—most notably burgeoning demand for AI infrastructure and tax incentives from the One Big Beautiful Bill, which allow companies to deduct 100% of eligible equipment and qualifying manufacturing-facility costs in the year placed in service, boosting near-term cash flow and after-tax returns.

Portfolio Update

Class A shares of the Alger Mid Cap Growth Fund outperformed the Russell Midcap Growth Index during the fourth quarter of 2025. Cidara Therapeutics, Inc. (CDTX), Natera, Inc. (NTRA), and Abivax (ABVX) were among the top contributors to performance.

  • Cidara Therapeutics is a biotechnology company developing drug-Fc conjugate (DFC) therapeutics, with its lead program CD388, an investigational long-acting, strain-agnostic antiviral designed to prevent influenza infection in individuals at higher risk of complications. CD388 was supported by compelling Phase 2b NAVIGATE results and advanced into the Phase 3 ANCHOR study, positioning it as a potentially first-in-class, single-dose prophylactic option for influenza prevention. Shares contributed positively after Merck announced an all-cash agreement to acquire Cidara for approximately $9.2 billion.
  • Natera is a specialty diagnostics laboratory that provides high-value genetic testing across three core franchises: reproductive health (including non-invasive prenatal testing for chromosomal conditions such as trisomy 13, 18, and 21), oncology (led by Signatera, which measures circulating tumor DNA to assess treatment response and detect molecular residual disease and cancer recurrence), and transplant (tests used to monitor organ rejection). These tests are built on the company's proprietary cell-free DNA liquid biopsy platform, enabling highly sensitive detection from a blood sample. Shares contributed positively after the company reported strong fiscal third-quarter earnings, with management commentary pointing to revenue and margin performance that exceeded expectations on the back of higher test volumes and favorable reimbursement dynamics—particularly within the Signatera franchise—alongside another increase to full-year 2025 revenue and margin guidance.
  • Abivax is a Paris-based, clinical-stage biotechnology company developing obefazimod, an oral, first-in-class miR-124 enhancer for inflammatory bowel disease, including ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease. Shares contributed positively to performance after the company reported pivotal top-line results from two large Phase 3 ulcerative colitis induction trials, which met key endpoints with statistically significant and clinically meaningful remission rates versus placebo and a favorable safety profile. Investor enthusiasm was further supported by the scale of the Phase 3 program and incremental follow-up data, while the prospect of potential regulatory filings beginning in 2026 helped reposition obefazimod as a credible late-stage asset rather than a speculative clinical story.

Roblox Corp. Class A (RBLX), e.l.f. Beauty, Inc. (ELF), and Nebius Group (NBIS) were among the top detractors from performance.

  • Roblox is a digital platform where users play, learn, communicate, and expand relationships in millions of 3D digital experiences within an open-world metaverse environment. The platform is powered by user-generated content (UGC) with experiences and most items being built by third-party developers and creators, on top of Roblox's development engine. Roblox uses AI to improve recommendations, moderate user-generated content, and help creators build new experiences more efficiently. The company reports bookings when users purchase its virtual currency, Robux, through one-time purchases, prepaid cards, or subscriptions. Shares detracted during the quarter following management's decision to use the surge in engagement and monetization to reinvest into the business through higher capital expenditures and operating expenses. The company plans to allocate capital into building and training their own AI models along with incorporating additional safeguards to weed out inappropriate activities.
  • e.l.f. Beauty is a multi-brand cosmetics and skincare company known for inclusive, clean, vegan, and cruelty-free products that deliver “prestige-like” quality at accessible, mass-market price points. Because the vast majority of its products are manufactured in China, the company has been more exposed than many peers to China-related tariffs, prompting e.l.f. to implement a $1 price increase to help offset higher costs. Shares detracted during the quarter after management highlighted a negative “ship-in versus sell-out” dynamic on its fiscal second-quarter earnings call, which led investors to reset near-term expectations.
  • Nebius is a provider of AI-focused cloud infrastructure, operating GPU compute capacity across Europe and expanding in the U.S. We view the company as a differentiated “neocloud” beneficiary of accelerating AI adoption, as demand for high-quality GPU compute continues to outstrip available supply of chips, power, and data center capacity. Nebius already operates a first-party data center in Finland and has deployed GPU capacity in Paris, while adding U.S. capacity through a Kansas City, Missouri deployment and a new data center build-out in New Jersey. During the quarter, shares detracted amid a broader selloff in AI infrastructure-related equities as investors grew more concerned about the “circularity” of AI ecosystem financing and the durability of AI infrastructure spending. Despite near-term volatility, Nebius continues to sign large, long-term agreements with tier-one customers, including multi-year AI infrastructure contracts with major cloud service providers, improving revenue visibility as new capacity comes online.

data.title: Weekly Market Pulse: Is The Stock Market Peaking?
data.description:
The Wall Street Journal published an article last week about the concentration of the S&P 500 and why you shouldn’t worry about it. The gist of the article is that the index has been more concentrated in the past, and calamity didn’t ensue, so it must be okay now. The author, Jason Zweig, is one of my favorite financial writers, so it stings a bit when he says:

Countless financial advisers and asset managers are saying the S&P 500 is “excessively concentrated,” “presents elevated risks to investors,” “is broken”, and is “not safe!”

He intimates that my and others’ concerns about the index are driven by marketing rather than investment purposes. That is certainly true of some in our business, like the doom and gloom crowd who want you to believe they can get you out at the top and back in at the bottom, which they reckon is a long way down from here and also from wherever the index was a year ago or 3 years ago or, in some cases, 15 years ago. Permabears are always going to find something to point to that seems very negative and requires you to hire them.

But I think in most cases, the analyses of the S&P 500’s concentration risk come from genuine concern. Zweig didn’t link to any of my articles about the subject, but he did link to articles by Morgan Stanley and Rockefeller Capital which were not written by those firms’ marketing departments. I’m sure they wrote about what they see as a genuine risk in “the market” if it is defined as the S&P 500. That’s the way I see it too; buying a highly valued, concentrated portfolio of stocks is riskier than owning a more diversified, less concentrated portfolio. My concern with this dismissal of that risk, by Mr. Zweig and plenty of other investors is that I worry they want to do the easy thing - just buy the S&P - because it’s been working. At least until recently.

Over the last six months, the S&P 500 ranks at the bottom of my above chart of random asset returns, most of which are just other equity index ETFs. US small-cap value, international value, international core, US large-cap value, midcaps, European stocks, and even REITs have all beaten the S&P 500. If you change the time frame to a year, the index climbs all the way to 8th place (out of 11). Of course, this is a short period of time and may not mean anything but it could also be the beginning of a longer period of underperformance. If it is, it certainly won’t be the first time; this has happened before. The last time the S&P 500 was so concentrated in the technology sector was during the dot com boom back at the turn of the century. In 2000, technology had grown to over a third of the index, and the result was severe underperformance in the ensuing period.

From the beginning of 2000 to the peak of the next market cycle, in mid-July of 2007, the S&P 500 total return averaged just 2.4% annually. That’s a total return of just 19.5% over a 7.5-year period. Mr. Zweig thinks you should just ride that out because, you know, the index does fine over the long term. The fact is that there were plenty of other stock market indexes that provided much better returns during that period. Small-cap stocks, the equal-weight S&P 500, large-cap value stocks, and foreign stocks all outperformed the S&P 500 during that period, and some of them by very large margins.

We can’t know the future, but we have to be clear-eyed about the present. The S&P 500 is concentrated in the technology and communications sectors. As constructed, it is expensive at about 24.5 times full-year 2026 estimates. That is well above the five-year average of 20, the ten-year average of 18.8 and way above the 25-year average of 16.3. Concentrated and expensive, and I’d say riskier than it would be with less concentration and a cheaper multiple. It is possible that the optimism embedded in the index today is justified. There is also some chance that it isn’t, that AI takes longer than expected to deliver its miracles. Since we don’t know which way things will come out, the logical thing to do is to spread our bets – diversify.

We don’t own the S&P 500 in our portfolios and haven’t for some time. That doesn’t mean that we don’t own technology stocks because we do; we just don’t have nearly 40% of our large-cap exposure in 10 stocks. We have found alternative indexes that provide us with large-cap exposure in a more diversified way. We also own small and midcap stocks as well as international stocks. They have all outperformed the S&P 500 over the last 3- and 6-month periods, and all but one have outperformed over the last year. The international index we own has outperformed the S&P 500 over the last three years.

The most recent bout of S&P 500 underperformance dates from October 29th last year, when the index was just a bit above where it is now. What stalled the index? Tech stocks stopped going up. Of Microsoft (MSFT), Nvidia (NVDA), Amazon (AMZN), Meta (META), Broadcom (AVGO), Palantir (PLTR), Oracle (ORCL), and Google (GOOG), only the last one is up since October 29th.

These aren’t the only stocks being affected by AI fears. Over the last couple of weeks, we’ve seen several sectors take hits on fears about how AI will affect their business. Software stocks have been particularly hard hit, with Intuit (INTU), Salesforce (CRM), ServiceNow (NOW), Adobe (ADBE), and Autodesk (ADSK) all down significantly from their highs. Apparently no one will buy software ever again because AI will just let them develop everything in-house. Insurance brokerage stocks such as AJ Gallagher (AJG), Brown & Brown (BRO), Willis Towers Watson (WTW), and AON (AON) were all hit because an online insurance shopping platform released a new app. Data companies like MSCI (MSCI) and S&P Global (SPGI) were knocked down because... well I’m not sure exactly why except they seem vulnerable somehow because... you know... AI.

In a sign that this may all be jumping the shark a bit, trucking stocks were hit hard last Thursday, with C. H. Robinson (CHRW) down nearly 25% at one point during the trading session, when a company called Algorhythm Holdings (RIME), which in a prior incarnation made karaoke machines, released a white paper which claimed that its SemiCab AI unit could boost freight volumes for customers by 300% “without a corresponding increase in operational headcount”. This is a penny stock company with a market cap of about $12 million, whose press release knocked billions off the value of trucking stocks. The irony is that these trucking companies would be the beneficiaries of such an app if it really existed. AI trading bots aren’t big on logic, I guess.

With some softer economic readings last week - retail sales came in weak, existing home sales disappointed - it’s tempting to layer an economic slowdown narrative on top of the AI fear trade. And the 10-year Treasury yield has fallen about 25 basis points since February 3rd, lending some superficial support to that story. But we haven’t really seen slowdown fears emerge in the market yet. The 10-year Treasury yield is down from its recent high, but rates were lower when stocks peaked in October. TIPS yields have moved a little too, but both are still in the same range they’ve been in for at least the last three years. Credit spreads have widened a little but are still very tight - 130 basis points tighter than three years ago.

The story that’s getting a bit buried here is that earnings season showed that growth has broadened beyond the tech sector. Overall, Q4 earnings are up over 13% - the fifth consecutive quarter of double-digit earnings growth - while revenue growth is running at over 9%. And it isn’t just tech anymore. 9 of 11 sectors are reporting earnings growth, up from 6 last quarter. Ironically, the sector with the worst recent stock performance was the one with the best earnings growth (technology) while one of the best-performing sectors was one of the two with earnings down (energy). Stock prices aren’t much interested in the present.

So, is the stock market peaking? Well, if you define the stock market as the S&P 500, it may be, although I’m more than a little skeptical. AI is a wonderful tool, and it may well impact a lot of industries. And some of those, like SaaS companies, may well need to adjust their business models, while some are rendered obsolete. But many will be enhanced by AI themselves and come out winners.

I would also note that while LLMs may be able to do a lot of the things humans can do, in the end it is still just aping human language. And like the karaoke machines once produced by Algorhythm Holdings (a clever nod to their music past, I must say) AI is only as good as its user. Have you ever been to Karaoke night? Imagine software written with the same level of skill as the average Karaoke singer.

Maybe I’m wrong and Jason Zweig is right that the S&P 500 today, with nearly 8% of the index in Nvidia, is just as safe as it was in 1932 when AT&T (T) was nearly 13% of the index. Sure, Nvidia is in a highly competitive industry, and AT&T was a protected monopoly in 1932 but that probably doesn’t matter. Right? But just in case it does, what are the three most important words in investing? Diversify, diversify, diversify.
